author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
Pelagius’ Behaviour Contrasted with that of the Writers of the Letter.
title: Pelagius’ Behaviour Contrasted with that of the Writers of the Letter.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Chapter 49 [XXV.]—Pelagius’ Behaviour Contrasted with that of the Writers of the Letter.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: If now that man, too, were to confess that he had once been implicated in this error as a person possessed, but that he now anathematized all that hold these opinions, whoever should withhold his congratulation from him, now that he was in possession of the way of truth, would surely surrender all the bowels of love.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: As the case, however, now stands, he has not only not acknowledged his liberation from his pestilential error; but, as if that were a small thing, he has gone on to anathematize men who have reached that freedom, who love him so well that they would fain desire his own emancipation.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Amongst these are those very men who have expressed their good-will towards him in the letter, which they forwarded to me.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: For he it was whom they had chiefly in view when they said how much they were affected at the fact of my having at last written that work.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: “If, indeed, it has happened,” they say, “that some are removed from the influence of this clearest light of truth, whose blindness required its illumination, yet even to them,” they go on to remark, “we doubt not, the self-same grace will find its way, by the merciful favour of God.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Any name, or names, even they, too, thought it desirable as yet to suppress, in order that, if friendship still lived on, the error of the friends might the more surely die.
